questions.q: What is the key difference between how an electric field and a magnetic field act on a charge?
options: An electric field only acts on moving charges; a magnetic field acts on stationary ones
A magnetic field only exerts a force on a moving charge; an electric field acts even on a stationary charge
Both fields only act on moving charges
Both fields act on stationary charges equally
questions.explanation: A stationary charge in an electric field still feels \(F = qE\), but a magnetic field exerts no force on a charge at rest — motion is the price of admission. This single fact (from \(F = qvB\sin\theta\) with \(v = 0\)) underlies the whole topic.

questions.q: The magnetic force on a charge moving at angle \(\theta\) to the field is \(F = qvB\sin\theta\). When is this force a maximum?
options: When the velocity is parallel to the field (\(\theta = 0^\circ\))
When the velocity is perpendicular to the field (\(\theta = 90^\circ\))
When the charge is stationary
When the velocity is at \(45^\circ\) to the field
questions.explanation: At \(\theta = 90^\circ\), \(\sin\theta = 1\) and \(F = qvB\) is maximum. At \(\theta = 0^\circ\) the velocity is along the field, \(\sin\theta = 0\), and the force vanishes. The trap is measuring the angle from the perpendicular instead of from the field.

questions.q: Why does a magnetic force never change the speed of a charged particle?
options: Because the force is too weak to do work
Because the force is always perpendicular to the velocity, so it does no work
Because the charge has no kinetic energy in a magnetic field
Because the field strength is constant
questions.explanation: Work requires a force component along the displacement. The magnetic force is always perpendicular to the velocity (the direction of motion), so it does no work. By the work-energy theorem, no work means no change in kinetic energy and therefore no change in speed — only direction changes.

questions.q: To find the direction of the magnetic force on a moving electron using the right-hand push rule, you must:
options: Use the rule unchanged, exactly as for a positive charge
Reverse the result, because the electron is negative
Point the thumb along the field instead of the velocity
Ignore the field direction entirely
questions.explanation: The right-hand push rule is built for positive charges. For a negative electron the force is the exact opposite of what the right hand gives, so you reverse the result (or point the thumb opposite to the electron's motion, or use the left hand). Reading the palm straight off for an electron is the most common error in this topic.

questions.q: Equating the magnetic force to the centripetal force gives the radius of the circular path. Which equation is the key step in this derivation?
options: \(qE = \dfrac{mv^2}{r}\)
\(qvB = \dfrac{mv^2}{r}\)
\(qvB = mv\)
questions.explanation: The magnetic force \(qvB\) provides the centripetal force \(mv^2/r\), so \(qvB = mv^2/r\). Cancelling one factor of \(v\) and rearranging gives \(r = mv/qB\). Stating that the magnetic force provides the centripetal force, and writing this line, is where the derivation marks are.

questions.q: According to \(r = mv/qB\), increasing the magnetic field strength \(B\) will:
options: Increase the radius, because a stronger push flings the particle wider
Decrease the radius, because a stronger inward force makes a tighter curve
Leave the radius unchanged
Reverse the direction of the circle
questions.explanation: \(B\) is in the denominator, so a stronger field gives a smaller radius. The force is centripetal (inward), so a bigger inward pull means a tighter curl, not a wider one. The trap is assuming more force means a bigger radius.

questions.q: An electron travelling at \(5.9\times10^{6}\ \mathrm{m\,s^{-1}}\) enters a field of 6.0 mT at right angles. What is the radius of its path? (\(m_e = 9.1\times10^{-31}\ \mathrm{kg}\), \(e = 1.6\times10^{-19}\ \mathrm{C}\))
questions.explanation: Convert 6.0 mT to \(6.0\times10^{-3}\ \mathrm{T}\). Then \(r = mv/qB = (9.1\times10^{-31})(5.9\times10^{6}) / [(1.6\times10^{-19})(6.0\times10^{-3})] = 5.6\times10^{-3}\ \mathrm{m}\) (5.6 mm). Forgetting the millitesla conversion is off by a factor of 1000.

questions.q: A proton and an electron enter the same field at the same speed, perpendicular to the field. How do their radii compare?
options: The radii are equal because charge and field are the same
The electron's radius is about 1836 times larger
The proton's radius is about 1836 times larger because it is more massive
The proton's radius is smaller because it carries more charge
questions.explanation: With \(r = mv/qB\) and equal speed, charge magnitude and field, the radius depends only on mass. The proton is about 1836 times more massive than the electron, so its radius is about 1836 times larger; the light electron whips round a much tighter circle.

questions.q: The period of circular motion in a magnetic field is \(T = 2\pi m / qB\). What does this tell us?
options: The period increases with the particle's speed
The period is independent of the particle's speed
The period decreases with the particle's speed
The period depends on the radius but not the mass
questions.explanation: Substituting \(r = mv/qB\) into \(T = 2\pi r/v\) makes \(v\) cancel, giving \(T = 2\pi m/qB\). A faster particle travels a proportionally larger circle, so the time per revolution is unchanged. This is the cyclotron principle. Note it is the period — not the radius — that is independent of speed.

questions.q: What is the trajectory of a charge moving at right angles to a uniform magnetic field, and how does it differ from the electric-field case?
options: A parabola, the same as in an electric field
A circle, whereas an electric field gives a parabola
A straight line, whereas an electric field gives a circle
A helix, whereas an electric field gives a straight line
questions.explanation: The perpendicular magnetic force constantly reorients to stay at right angles to the velocity, giving uniform circular motion. The electric force is constant in direction (like gravity), giving a parabola. Writing "parabola" for a uniform magnetic field is a lost mark.
